mnemonics.title: P.A.C.M.F. - OS Resource Functions
mnemonics.content: P.A.C.M.F. = Process management, Allocation, Control, Memory management, File management. The OS acts as a manager for all computer resources ensuring efficient utilization.

mnemonics.title: M.A.N. - Memory Allocation Needs
mnemonics.content: M.A.N. = Memory allocation, Address translation, Nullification (garbage collection). Remember the three key aspects of memory management in OS.

mnemonics.title: C.P.U. - Central Processing Unit Scheduling
mnemonics.content: C.P.U. = Compact Processing Unit. CPU is the heart of resource management - the OS schedules which process gets CPU time using various algorithms.

mnemonics.title: D.E.A.D. - Deadlock Conditions
mnemonics.content: D.E.A.D. = Deadlock, Exclusion, Allocation, Demand. Four necessary conditions: Mutual Exclusion, Hold and Wait, No Preemption, Circular Wait.

mnemonics.title: S.I.X. R's of I/O Management
mnemonics.content: S.I.X. = Scheduling, Isolation, Interleaving, Xenial (device-friendly), Xecution (execution control). Remember the goals of I/O device management.

acronyms.usage: CPU scheduling algorithm where the process that arrives first gets CPU time first. Simplest but may cause long waiting times.
CPU scheduling that selects the process with smallest burst time. Optimal for minimizing average waiting time but requires knowing execution time in advance.
CPU scheduling where each process gets equal time slice (quantum) in cyclic order. Fair algorithm used in time-sharing systems.
Data structure storing all information about a process (state, PC, registers, memory map, I/O status). The OS uses PCB to manage processes.
Page replacement algorithm that replaces the oldest page in memory. Simple but not always optimal.
Page replacement algorithm that evicts page not used for longest time. Good approximation of optimal algorithm.
Allows I/O devices to transfer data directly to/from memory without CPU intervention, improving system efficiency.
Hardware cache storing recent virtual-to-physical address translations for faster memory access.

visualTips.visualization: Imagine OS as the manager of a busy restaurant kitchen. The chef (CPU) needs ingredients (data), tools (devices), workspace (memory), and recipes (programs). The manager (OS) coordinates everything ensuring no resource is overused or idle.

Picture a hospital emergency room triage. Patients (processes) arrive with different urgency levels (priority). The nurse (scheduler) decides who sees the doctor (CPU) next based on severity and waiting time.

Visualize a large library (memory) with a librarian (OS) who allocates shelves (memory blocks) to students (processes). The librarian keeps track of which shelves are free/occupied and prevents students from accessing each other's books.
Think of two people at a deadlock: Person A has fork, needs knife; Person B has knife, needs fork. Both wait forever. This is circular wait - one of the four conditions for deadlock.

Imagine a relay race runner (process) passing the baton (CPU state) to next runner. The baton transfer is like context switch - saving all information about the current runner and preparing the next one.

analogies.analogy: Process management is like a school principal managing students. Each student is a process with its own identity (PCB), schedule, and resources. The principal (OS) creates new students (fork), monitors their activities (execute), and dismisses them when done (terminate).

Memory management is like a hotel manager allocating rooms to guests. The hotel has fixed rooms (physical memory), guests request rooms of certain sizes (memory requests), and the manager must keep track of occupied/free rooms (allocation/deallocation) and prevent guests from entering others' rooms (protection).

Device management is like traffic control at an airport. Airplanes (I/O devices) arrive at different times, the control tower (OS) schedules takeoffs/landings, assigns runways (device access), and ensures no collisions occur.

CPU scheduling is like a judge managing a courtroom. Multiple lawyers (processes) want to speak (CPU time). The judge uses specific rules (scheduling algorithms) to decide who speaks when, for how long, and ensures everyone gets a fair chance.

File management is like a librarian organizing books in a library. The librarian (OS) maintains the catalog (file system), ensures proper shelving (storage), and controls who can read/modify which books (file permissions).
